source_metadata.title_raw: Jessica Morsell-Haye - A-20-022 - March 13, 2020 - Fort Bragg

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 13, 2020

Jessica Morsell-Haye
Fort Bragg City Councilmember
360 N. Whipple Street
Fort Bragg, CA 95437

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-20-022

Dear Ms. Morsell-Haye:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Finally, nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place, and any conclusions drawn apply only to prospective actions. (See Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A).)

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, a Fort Bragg City Councilmember and a member of the City's Mill Site Rezoning Ad Hoc Committee, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the potential rezoning of a 420-acre, ocean-front real property that is currently the site of a decommissioned lumber mill (the "Mill Site") given that you have financial interests in businesses and real property located between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Mill Site?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site because those decisions' financial effect on your financial interests in the businesses and real property at issue would be indistinguishable from the decisions' effect on the public generally based on the facts presented.

_____________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-20-022
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The City of Fort Bragg is located on California's Mendocino Coast, approximately 165
miles north of San Francisco and 188 miles west of Sacramento, and the City's total area is 2.77
square miles. You are a Fort Bragg City Councilmember and a member of the City's Mill Site
Rezoning Ad Hoc Committee.

The "Mill Site" is a 420-acre, ocean-front real property that is currently occupied by a
decommissioned lumber mill. The lumber mill employed about 2,000 people at the peak of its
operations, and its closure substantially affected the local economy. The Mill Site Rezoning Ad Hoc
Committee is charged with reconciling proposed zoning maps from the Planning Commission and
City Council with community feedback and presenting its recommendations to the City Council.
Upon receipt of the Committee's recommendations, the City Council will determine whether to
rezone the Mill Site.

You, your husband, and your father are members of two separate limited liability companies
("LLCs") located within the City: M&H Investments LLC ("M&H") and Golden West Sales LLC
("Golden West"). Both M&H and Golden West do business out of a commercial real property
located on East Redwood Avenue between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Mill Site (the "East
Redwood Property"). M&H manages long-term rentals, owns the East Redwood Property, and
operates out of the East Redwood Property's top two floors. You and your husband each have a 20-
percent stake, and your father has a 40-percent stake, in M&H. Golden West is a bar and restaurant
which operates out of the East Redwood Property's first floor and has been in business since the
1890s. You and your father each have a 25-percent stake, and your husband has a 30-percent stake,
in Golden West.

There are 438 businesses located within the City, and 238 of those businesses (or 54
percent) are located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site. You state that: the majority of the City's
commercial activity occurs within the City's Central Business District, along the Highway I/Main
Street Corridor immediately east of the Mill Site; any development of the Mill Site would impact
nearly all businesses and residents within the City; and the Mill Site's development would
financially affect both M&H and Golden West. Because the Central Business District is primarily
comprised of bars, restaurants, retail establishments, and long-term rentals, you assert that the
decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site would not have a unique financial effect
on either M&H or Golden West compared to those decisions' effect on other businesses within the
District.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision would have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial
interests distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and
87103.) An official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest are
set forth in Section 87103 and include the following:

File No. A-20-022
Page No. 3

• A business interest in any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any management position (Section 87103(d)).

• A real property interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• A source of income interest in any source of income from whom the official receives $500 or more in value in the 12 months prior to when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

• A source of gift interest in any donor, or the donor's intermediary or an agent, from whom the official receives a gift or gifts worth $500 or more in value in the 12 months prior to when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances including those the official's spouse and dependent children. (Sections 82029 and 87103.)

Of those financial interests, you have the following with respect to decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site based on the facts presented: (1) business interests in M&H and Golden West assuming your investment in each business is worth $2,000 or more; (2) a real property interest in the East Redwood Property owned by M&H assuming your interest in that property is worth $2,000 or more;² (3) source of income interests in M&H, Golden West, or any client of either M&H or Golden West if you receive $500 or more in income from that source of income in the 12 months prior to such a decision;³ and (4) an interest in your personal finances.⁴

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a governmental decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the

_______________
² Section 82033 provides that an individual's "interests in real property" include "a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly or indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." Because you have greater than a 10-percent interest in M&H, you have a real property interest in the East Redwood Property, assuming your interest in that property is worth $2,000 or more.

³ Section 82030 provides that an individual's "income" includes "a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." Because you have greater than a 10-percent interest in both M&H and Golden West, you would have a source of income interest in any client of either or both of those businesses from whom you receive $500 or more in income in the 12 months prior to the decision at issue.

⁴ Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances is not considered separately if the effect is related to the decision's effect on the official's business interests or real property interest. The facts presented provide no indication that decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site would have any effect on your interest in your personal finances separate from those related to your business interests in M&H and Golden West or your real property interest in the East Redwood Property. Therefore, we do not further analyze those decisions' effect on your interest in your personal finances.

File No. A-20-022
Page No. 4

decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. In addition, an official's business interest is
explicitly involved in any decision affecting the business as described in Regulation 18702.1(a)(1),
and an official's real property interest is explicitly involved in any decision affecting real
property as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) through (6).

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect on
such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical."

The facts presented provide no indication that any of your interests are explicitly involved in
decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site.

M&H and Golden West

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's source of income interest. With respect to such an effect
on an official's not explicitly involved source of income interest in a business, Regulation
18702.3(a)(4) provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1.⁵

Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's inexplicitly involved business interest, and provides that
the decision's effect is material if:

• The decision may result in an increase or decrease of the business's annual gross revenues, or
the value of the business's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000,
or five percent of the business's annual gross revenues and the increase or decrease is at least
$10,000. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(2).)

• The decision may cause the business to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate expenses by equal to or more than $250,000, or one percent of the business's annual
gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $2,500. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).)

The Mill Site is a 420-acre, ocean-front real property located directly west of the City's
Central Business District that is currently occupied by a decommissioned lumber mill. The lumber
mill once employed approximately 2,000 people, and its closure substantially affected the local
economy. You state that any development of the Mill Site would impact all the City's businesses
and residents. If decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site enable its development
and lead to associated increase in traffic levels and intensity of use within the Central Business

⁵ Regulation 18702.3(b) sets forth the "Nexus Test," an alternative basis for a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a source of income interest to be determined material, which provides that "[a]ny
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a source of income to a public official or the official's spouse is material if
the decision will achieve, defeat, aid, or hinder a purpose or goal of the source and the official or the official's spouse
receives or is promised the income for achieving the purpose or goal." Based on the facts presented, there is no
indication that the Nexus Test applies to the effect of decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site on
M&H, Golden West, or a client of one or both of those businesses who qualifies as a source of income interest.

File No. A-20-022
Page No. 5

District, then those decisions may result in an increase in M&H or Golden West's annual gross
revenues. Those decisions' reasonably foreseeable financial effect on M&H or Golden West would
be material under Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) if they result in an increase of that business's annual
gross revenues in an amount equal to more than $1,000,000, or five percent of that business's
annual gross revenues if the increase or decrease is at least $10,000.

M&H has a real property interest in the East Redwood Property because it owns that
property, and you state that M&H has that interest. The East Redwood Property is located within
the Central Business District, between 500 to 1,000 feet from the Mill Site. Decisions relating to the
potential rezoning of the Mill Site may enable the Mill Site's development, and development of
such a large real property within 500 to 1,000 feet of the East Redwood Property could substantially
alter the East Redwood Property's traffic levels, intensity of use, or market value. Furthermore,
intensity of use and market value may change the East Redwood Property's income-
producing potential as a commercial real property. The facts presented provide a strong indication
that decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site would have a substantial effect on
the East Redwood Property. If that is the case, it would be reasonably foreseeable that those
decisions would have a material financial effect on your business interest in M&H under Regulation
18702.1(a)(4).

M&H and Golden West Clients

As noted above, with respect to a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an
official's source of income interest in a business, Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that materiality
is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. If the official has a source of income interest in an
individual, then Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) sets forth circumstances that determine whether the effect
on that individual is material.

Because the facts presented do not provide any indication that decisions relating to the
potential rezoning of the Mill Site would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any
particular client of M&H or Golden West who qualifies as a source of income interest, we do not
further analyze those decisions' effect on any client of M&H or Golden West.

East Redwood Property

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's real property interest. Subdivision (a)(7) of that
regulation provides that such an effect is material if the decision involves property located more
than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line of the parcel, and the decision would
change the parcel's development potential, income-producing potential, highest and best use,
character by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise
levels, air quality, or market value.

The East Redwood Property is located between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Mill Site.
Decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site could enable its development. You state
that any development of the Mill Site would impact all the City's businesses and residents. Given
the size of the Mill Site and its proximity to the East Redwood Property, those decisions could
change the East Redwood Property's income-producing potential and substantially alter the East

File No. A-20-022
Page No. 6

Redwood Property's traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, air quality, or market value. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on your real property interest in the East Redwood Property under Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).

Thus, the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site unless an exception applies.

Public Generally Exception

Regulation 18703 sets forth the Public Generally Exception, and subdivision (a) of that regulation provides the general rule: "A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial interests is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment." Regulation 18703(b) defines "significant segment of the public" as 25 percent of:

• All business or nonprofit entities within the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b)(1).)

• All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b)(2).)

• All individuals within the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b)(3).)

There are 438 businesses located within the City; and 238 of those businesses (54 percent), including M&H and Golden West, are located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site. Although you did not provide the total number of commercial real properties located within the City or the number of commercial real properties located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site, based on the fact that 54 percent of the City's total businesses are located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site we are assuming that at least 25 percent of all the City's commercial real properties are located within that area, like the East Redwood Property. Therefore, based on the facts presented, businesses and commercial real properties located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site each constitute a "significant segment of the public" for purposes of the Public Generally Exception.

You state that any development of the Mill Site following its potential rezoning would impact all businesses and residents within the City, and that those decisions would not have a unique effect on M&H or Golden West compared to their effect on all other businesses within the Central Business District because the District is primarily comprised of bars, restaurants, retail establishments, and long-term rentals. Furthermore, the facts presented provide no indication of a unique effect on M&H, Golden West, or the East Redwood Property compared to the relevant significant segment. Therefore, based on the facts presented, those decisions would not have a "unique effect" on any of your interests at issue for purposes of the Public Generally Exception.

Thus, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site because those decisions' effect on your interests at issue would be indistinguishable from their effect on the public generally based on the facts presented.

File No. A-20-022
Page No. 7

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:sal

decommissioned lumber mill (the "Mill Site") given that you have financial interests in businesses 
and real property located between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Mill Site?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential 
rezoning of the Mill Site because those decisions' financial effect on your financial interests in the 
businesses and real property at issue would be indistinguishable from the decisions' effect on the 
public generally based on the facts presented.

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decision would have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial 
interests distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 
87103.) An official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest are 
set forth in Section 87103 and include the following:  
• 
A business interest in any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment 
worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any management position (Section 87103(d)). 
• 
A real property interest in any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest 
worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

official receives a gift or gifts worth $500 or more in value in the 12 months prior to when the 
decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances including those the official's spouse and 
dependent children. (Sections 82029 and 87103.)

Golden West assuming your investment in each business is worth $2,000 or more; (2) a real 
property interest in the East Redwood Property owned by M&H assuming your interest in that 
property is worth $2,000 or more;2 (3) source of income interests in M&H, Golden West, or any 
client of either M&H or Golden West if you receive $500 or more in income from that source of 
income in the 12 months prior to such a decision;3 and ( 4) an interest in your personal finances. 4 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that a governmental decision's financial effect on an official's 
financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly 
involved" in the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named 
party in, or the subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the

in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or

M&H has a real property interest in the East Redwood Property because it owns that 
property, and you know that M&H has that interest. The East Redwood Property is located within 
the Central Business District, between 500 to 1,000 feet from the Mill Site. Decisions relating to the 
potential rezoning of the Mill Site may enable the Mill Site's development, and development of 
such a large real property within 500 to 1,000 feet of the East Redwood Property could substantially 
alter the East Redwood Property's traffic levels, intensity of use, or market value. Furthermore, 
increased intensity of use and market value may change the East Redwood Property's income-
producing potential as a commercial real property. The facts presented provide a strong indication 
that decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site would have a substantial effect on 
the East Redwood Property. If that is the case, it would be reasonably foreseeable that those 
decisions would have a material financial effect on your business interest in M&H under Regulation 
18702.l(a)(4). 
M&H and Golden West Clients 
As noted above, with respect to a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an 
official's source of income interest in a business, Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that materiality 
is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. If the official has a source of income interest in an 
individual, then Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) sets forth circumstances that determine whether the effect 
on that individual is material. 
Because the facts presented do not provide any indication that decisions relating to the 
potential rezoning of the Mill Site would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any 
particular client of M&H or Golden West who qualifies as a source of income interest, we do not 
further analyze those decisions' effect on any client of M&H or Golden West. 
East Redwood Property 
Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect on an official's real property interest. Subdivision (a)(7) of that 
regulation provides that such an effect is material if the decision involves property located more 
than 500 feet but less than 1,000 feet from the property line of the parcel, and the decision would 
change the parcel's development potential, income-producing potential, highest and best use, 
character by substantially altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise 
levels, air quality, or market value. 
The East Redwood Property is located between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Mill Site. 
Decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site could enable its development. You state 
that any development of the Mill Site would impact all the City's businesses and residents. Given 
the size of the Mill Site and its proximity to the East Redwood Property, those decisions could 
change the East Redwood Property's income-producing potential and substantially alter the East  
Redwood Property's traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, air quality, 
or market value. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material 
financial effect on your real property interest in the East Redwood Property under Regulation 
l 8702.2(a)(7). 
Thus, the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential rezoning of 
the Mill Site unless an exception applies. 
Public Generally Exception 
Regulation 18703 sets forth the Public Generally Exception, and subdivision (a) of that 
regulation provides the general rule: "A governmental decision's financial effect on a public 
official's financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial 
interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment." Regulation 18703(b) 
defines "significant segment of the public" as 25 percent of: 
• 
All business or nonprofit entities within the jurisdiction. (Regulation l 8703(b )(1 ).) 
• 
All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within the jurisdiction. 
(Regulation 18703(b )(2).) 
• 
All individuals within the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b)(3).) 
There are 438 businesses located within the City; and 238 of those businesses (54 percent), 
including M&H and Golden West, are located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site. Although you did 
not provide the total number of commercial real properties located within the City or the number of 
commercial properties located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site, based on the fact that 54 percent of 
the City's total businesses are located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site we are assuming that at least 
25 percent of all the City's commercial real properties are located within that area, like the East 
Redwood Property. Therefore, based on the facts presented, businesses and commercial real 
properties located within 1,000 feet of the Mill Site each constitute a "significant segment of the 
public" for purposes of the Public Generally Exception. 
You state that any development of the Mill Site following its potential rezoning would 
impact all businesses and residents within the City, and that those decisions would not have a 
unique effect on M&H or Golden West compared to their effect on all other businesses within the 
Central Business District because the District is primarily comprised of bars, restaurants, retail 
establishments, and long-term rentals. Furthermore, the facts presented provide no indication of a 
unique effect on M&H, Golden West, or the East Redwood Property compared to the relevant 
significant segment. Therefore, based on the facts presented, those decisions would not have a 
"unique effect" on any of your interests at issue for purposes of the Public Generally Exception. 
Thus, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential 
rezoning of the Mill Site because those decisions' effect on your interests at issue would be 
indistinguishable from their effect on the public generally based on the facts presented.

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit you, a Fort Bragg City Councilmember and a member of the City's 
Mill Site Rezoning Ad Hoc Committee, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the 
potential rezoning of a 420-acre, ocean-front real property that is currently the site of a 
decommissioned lumber mill (the "Mill Site") given that you have financial interests in businesses 
and real property located between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Mill Site?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential 
rezoning of the Mill Site because those decisions' financial effect on your financial interests in the 
businesses and real property at issue would be indistinguishable from the decisions' effect on the 
public generally based on the facts presented.

Whipple Street Fort Bragg, CA 95437 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-20-022 Dear Ms. Morsell-Haye: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. Finally, nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place, and any conclusions drawn apply only to prospective actions. (See Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A).) QUESTION Does the Act prohibit you, a Fort Bragg City Councilmember and a member of the City's Mill Site Rezoning Ad Hoc Committee, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the potential rezoning of a 420-acre, ocean-front real property that is currently the site of a decommissioned lumber mill (the "Mill Site") given that you have financial interests in businesses and real property located between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Mill Site? CONCLUSION No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the potential rezoning of the Mill Site because those decisions' financial effect on your financial interests in the businesses and real property at issue would be indistinguishable from the decisions' effect on the public generally based on the facts presented. _____________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-20-022 Page No. 2 FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER The City of Fort Bragg is located on California's Mendocino Coast, approximately 165 miles north of San Francisco and 188 miles west of Sacramento, and the City's total area is 2.77 square miles. You are a Fort Bragg City Councilmember and a member of the City's Mill Site Rezoning Ad Hoc Committee. The "Mill Site" is a 420-acre, ocean-front real property that is currently occupied by a decommissioned lumber mill. The lumber mill employed about 2,000 people at the peak of its operations, and its closure substantially affected the local economy. The Mill Site Rezoning Ad
